coredata.dc:title: Visual detection of biogenic amines in foods - Current technologies, challenges, and future prospects

Luo, Lin
Huang, Guidong
Zhou, Kai
Xu, Zhen-Lin
coredata.dc:description:
Background Biogenic amines in food have gotten a lot of attention because they could be harmful. This shows how important strong detection methods are for making sure food is safe. As the need for quick and on-site detection methods grows, visual methods for biogenic amine analysis have become essential tools because they work quickly, are easy to understand, and are very sensitive. Consequently, visual detection approaches have witnessed progressive development and widespread adoption in recent years.
Scope and approach This review provides a thorough examination of recent research developments in visual detection techniques for biogenic amines. The discourse involves the application of coloring agents and signaling modalities for visual detection, alongside the burgeoning uses of smartphone-based intelligent sensing platforms in this domain. Moreover, specific attention is directed towards tackling the challenges and opportunities associated with visual detection techniques for biogenic amines. The review's aim is to highlight the research and development of visual detection methods for biogenic amines.
Key findings and conclusions: Among the detection methods for biogenic amines, visual sensor analysis has emerged as one of the most rapidly advancing techniques, characterized by its short analysis time, simplicity of operation, and adaptability to on-site measurements. The rapid progress in responsive materials and signal modes has significantly improved the sensitivity and sensing performance of visual analysis techniques. The application of smartphone-based smart detection platforms, particularly the integration with machine learning, in the rapid visual detection methods represents a significant milestone towards achieving rapid and on-site detection of biogenic amines in foods. Furthermore, the integration of research findings from various disciplines into the design and optimization of vision sensors is essential for future development.

Partial typical colorimetric sensors for biogenic amine detection in examples. (A) Colorimetric films derived from sugarcane for visual monitoring of the biogenic amines in seafood. Reprinted with permission from Ref (Wang et al., 2023), copyright 2023 Elsevier; (B) Rapid detection of biogenic amines using bromothymol blue-based colorimetric microneedle patches. Reprinted with permission from Ref (Wang, Chen, et al., 2022), copyright 2022 Elsevier; (C) Hydrogel beads based on polydiacetylene as colorimetric sensors for detecting biogenic amines in spoiled meat. Reprinted with permission from Ref (Jang et al., 2023), copyright 2022 Elsevier; (D) Gold nanoparticles used for colorimetric detection of food freshness through amine-responsive dopamine polymerization. Reprinted with permission from Ref (Li et al., 2022), copyright 2021 Elsevier; (E) Multicolorimetric sensing of histamine in fishes based on enzymatic etching of gold nanorods. Reprinted with permission from Ref (Xu et al., 2022), copyright 2021 Elsevier. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Partial typical fluorescence sensors for biogenic amine detection in examples. Reprinted with permission from Ref (Liu, Jiang, et al., 2022), copyright 2021 Elsevier; (B) Schematic diagram illustrating the utilization of carbon quantum dots/specificity antibodies for histamine detection. (a) Schematic depiction of a validation test based on carbon quantum dots using candidate peptides. (b) Proposed assay scheme for histamine detection in real sample. Detail of different signal modes, mechanisms, properties of the visual sensors.

row.entry: Signal modes
Mechanisms
Advantages
Others
Ref.
Colorimetric mode
The reaction of biogenic amines with other substances to produce noticeable color changes to qualitatively and/or quantitatively detect biogenic amines.

·Simple operation
·Low cost
·On site
·Real-time
·Non-destructive

·Qualitative and semiquantitative
·Cannot be used beyond acertain concentration
·Easy to cross reaction
·High-throughput detection
·Coloring materials from natural and synthetic sources
·Materials with high binding enthalpies (∼40 to ∼300 kJ/mol) have high sensitivity
Fluorescence mode
The interaction between binding units (or receptors) and biogenic amines, which would change the fluorescence signal through different communication mechanisms.

·Satisfactory sensitivity
·Fast response
·High-throughput
·Reduced background signals
·Ease of automation
·Non-destructive

·Chemical derivatization
·Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
·High-throughput detection
·In situ detection

Heinemann et al., 2022
Wei et al. (2024)
Chemiluminescence mode
A chemical reaction is generated in an electronically excited state and returns to its ground state under emission of light.

·Assembly like quickness
·Easy operation
·Simple procedure
·Low detection limit
·No need monochromator

·Different light resource (normally in the visible and/or near-infrared regions)
·Chemiluminescent sensors can response to few analytes
·Chemiluminescence can be divided into direct luminescence, catalyzed luminescence, induced luminescence, etc

Ahangari et al. (2021)
Danchuk et al. (2020)
Vasconcelos et al., 2021

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biogenic amines in food have gotten a lot of attention because they could be harmful. This shows how important strong detection methods are for making sure food is safe. As the need for quick and on-site detection methods grows, visual methods for biogenic amine analysis have become essential tools because they work quickly, are easy to understand, and are very sensitive. Consequently, visual detection approaches have witnessed progressive development and widespread adoption in recent years.

This review provides a thorough examination of recent research developments in visual detection techniques for biogenic amines. The discourse involves the application of coloring agents and signaling modalities for visual detection, alongside the burgeoning uses of smartphone-based intelligent sensing platforms in this domain. Moreover, specific attention is directed towards tackling the challenges and opportunities associated with visual detection techniques for biogenic amines. The review's aim is to highlight the research and development of visual detection methods for biogenic amines.

Key findings and conclusions: Among the detection methods for biogenic amines, visual sensor analysis has emerged as one of the most rapidly advancing techniques, characterized by its short analysis time, simplicity of operation, and adaptability to on-site measurements. The rapid progress in responsive materials and signal modes has significantly improved the sensitivity and sensing performance of visual analysis techniques. The application of smartphone-based smart detection platforms, particularly the integration with machine learning, in the rapid visual detection methods represents a significant milestone towards achieving rapid and on-site detection of biogenic amines in foods. Furthermore, the integration of research findings from various disciplines into the design and optimization of vision sensors is essential for future development.

ce:section.ce:para: Unsafe food raises global concerns and creates trade barriers, endangering public health and the resilience of food systems worldwide. According to World Health Organization estimates, one in every ten people suffer from diseases caused by unsafe food containing food-risk components, accounting for 420,000 deaths each year (Zhang et al., 2024). Among the contributing factors to food safety issues, biogenic amines have been identified as a major cause of food safety concerns (Wu et al., 2022). This is supported by European Food Safety Authority (EFSA)'s 2017 assessment report on histamine poisoning events between 2010 and 2014, which documented 306 foodborne outbreaks across 12 EU member states (Yu et al., 2024). Biogenic amines are low molecular weight alkaline nitrogen-containing compounds with biological activity that are formed through amino acid decarboxylation or aldehyde and ketone amination and transamination reactions (Schirone et al., 2022). Consumption of foods high in biogenic amines has been linked to side effects such as vomiting, respiratory problems, and food poisoning (Liu, Chen, et al., 2024). Histamine is considered one of the most potent and well-studied among various toxic biogenic amines (Hungerford, 2010). It has been reported that HT29 cells exhibited an IC50 value of 26 mM, and doses of histamine at 10.8 mM or above were found to demonstrate cytotoxicity (Linares et al., 2016). Furthermore, studies have shown that the consumption of 0.75 mg/kg body weight of histamine or food including 50 mg/kg of histamine could cause toxicity in susceptible individuals (Gagic et al., 2019). The amount consumed as well as personal sensitivity to every biogenic amine determine the overall degree of poisoning resulting from them (Danchuk et al., 2020). Recent studies have concentrated on tracking biogenic amines in foodstuffs to reduce the incidence of human diseases given the possible health hazards connected with them and the limited efficacy of methods such thermal approaches and UV radiation in suppressing their production (Guizani et al., 2005).

Chromatography techniques, including gas chromatography (GC) (Wojnowski et al., 2019), ion exchange chromatography (IEC) (Jastrzȩbska et al., 2015), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Tsai et al., 2021), and related hyphenated techniques such as ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography combined with mass spectrometry (MS) (Du et al., 2023) and capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Gubartallah et al., 2018), have conventionally been employed as detection methods for biogenic amines, providing accurate and reliable results. Among these methods, HPLC has been widely utilized due to its frequent usage and high accuracy in biogenic amine detection, making it the official method (AOAC method 977.13) for analyzing biogenic amines in food samples (Gao et al., 2023). However, these traditional approaches rely highly on expensive equipment and time-consuming processes, greatly limiting their applicability in field settings (Lin et al., 2016). In this sense, there is an urgent need to develop rapid, cost-effective, and on-site methods for determining biogenic amines.

In recent decades, visual sensors have garnered increasing attention due to their straightforward operation, rapid analysis time, high sensitivity, visualization capabilities, among others (Li, Gan, et al., 2021). The design of these sensors has concentrated on the crucial use of coloring materials. Moreover, the development of visual sensors with diverse signal modes in recent years has expanded their application range and improved detection performance. Furthermore, the proliferation of portable/hand-held intelligent devices like smartphones enables on-site detection of biogenic amines (Yousefi et al., 2019). This comprehensive review presents the applications of coloring materials in visual detection of biogenic amines while also providing a categorized description of visual sensor types including single-mode (colorimetric sensor, fluorometric sensor, chemiluminescent sensor) and multiple-mode sensors. Additionally, the latest applications utilizing smartphone-based smart sensing platforms for biogenic amine visual detection were also reviewed and outlined (Fig. 1 ). Finally, the current challenges and future perspectives were emphasized while providing an extensive analysis of visual sensor characteristics as well as their merits/demerits and demerits to assist in the design of new devices for reliable, intelligent, and fast analytics. The objective of this survey is to present a comprehensive overview of the latest research progress in the visual detection of biogenic amines in the food field and intends to inspire the development of more accurate and efficient biogenic amine detection strategies and techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of different coloring material used in biogenic amine visual detection
ce:section.ce:para: Due to escalating concerns regarding food safety, there has been a significant emphasis on developing economic, rapid, and sensitive strategies for monitoring biogenic amines determination. Visual sensors have become highly appealing due to their affordability, disposability, as well as the prompt and straightforward interpretation of their signal responses (Mazur et al., 2024). The incorporation of coloring materials plays a pivotal role in enhancing sensing performance. This section will provide an overview of various coloring materials along with their respective advantages and disadvantages in detecting biogenic amines.

As universally acknowledged, biogenic amines are alkaline compounds generated through the enzymatic decarboxylation of amino acids (Moniente et al., 2022). In this context, color-changing sensors that respond to pH variations in the medium offer distinct advantageous as they can be easily perceived by the naked eye. These non-destructive and instrument-free sensors make use of pH-sensitive materials (Gomes Müller et al., 2022), which are commonly applied for sensor design.

pH-sensitive dyes, encompassing both natural and synthetic variants, are widely employed as the predominant pH-sensitive materials for biogenic amine detection. The color change mechanism of pH-sensitive materials can be chemically elucidated by protonation/deprotonation reactions occurring at active sites (Gomes Müller et al., 2022). Bromothymol blue (Sutthasupa et al., 2021), bromocresol green (Wen et al., 2023), and methyl red (Ayu Sariningsih et al., 2019) represent the most commonly applied chemical dyes for biogenic amine detection. In recent years, an increasing number of researchers have turned to biocompatible natural dyes such as anthocyanins (Faisal et al., 2024), curcumin (Yang, Chen, et al., 2024), and alizarin (Ezati et al., 2020) as coloring agents to achieve non-toxicity and environmental friendliness. In addition, modifying pH-sensitive dyes to improve their response performance has emerged as a prominent research focus in recent times. For example, hydroxyanthraquinones extracted from Rubiaceae are a class of pH-sensitive substances. The phenolic hydroxyl group can undergo dissociation in an alkaline environment, resulting in significant changes in the molecule's conjugated structure and subsequent alteration in color (Ramamoorthy et al., 2009). In Wang et al.’s study (Wang et al., 2021), a series of hydroxyanthraquinone derivatives containing carboxyl and methyl groups were synthesized through Friedel-Crafts alkylation reaction to enhance their pH response performance. The findings showed that the electron-withdrawing substituents can augment the pH sensitivity of hydroxyanthraquinone compounds. Although these base pigments sensitive to pH can detect the presence of biogenic amines, they can only provide qualitative and semi-quantitative information.

In addition to pH-sensitive pigments, certain polymers that are sensitive to changes in pH can induce structural modifications that give rise to alterations of color, such as polydiacetylene and polyaniline. For instance, the color of polydiacetylene transitions from blue to red upon exposure to varying pH levels. The blue hue originates from the π-π∗ transition of electrons within the conjugated main chain (Seo et al., 2021), which experiences an increased energy gap when influenced by external factors (Son et al., 2021). Consequently, this leads to a red coloration due to the absorption of high-energy wavelengths within the visible light spectrum. Moreover, researchers have also developed alternative materials responsive to changes in pH. For example, Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2022) synthesized a donor-acceptor structured fluorogen exhibiting both aggregation-induced emission activity and sensitivity towards pH variations. Upon stimulation by changes in pH levels, protonation/deprotonation processes occur within the donor/acceptor moiety, resulting in weakened/enhanced interactions between them, respectively. Consequently, this leads to emissions with shifted colors (blue/red) and fluorescence generation.

Apart from pH-sensitive dyes, amine-sensitive chromophores are commonly used as direct discoloration materials for visual detection of biogenic amines. The analyte destructs the chromophore core, resulting in the loss of π-conjugation and significant alterations in optical properties (Bhalla et al., 2010; Yang, Seidlits, et al., 2010). Therefore, materials based on chromophore reaction have the advantage of a broad ultraviolet–visible spectrum and emission spectrum shift, enabling colorimetric and fluorescence dual signal realization through the naked eye visualization process (Du, Hu, Fan, & Peng, 2012). Boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) and its derivatives represent the most frequently utilized amine-sensitive chromophore materials (Li, Li, Ran, et al., 2019; Li, Li, et al., 2020; Wang, Ding, et al., 2020). For instance, Wang et al. (Wang, Song, et al., 2020) developed an amine-sensitive chromophore material based on lactam fusion of aza-BODIPY. The chromophoric reaction mainly involves the cleavage of polyamine B-N bonds and rapid hydrolysis to generate smaller conjugated molecules. Its unique capability in detecting polyamines enables quick identification of their presence in spoiled fish. Furthermore, numerous novel materials have been synthesized as well. Sui et al. (Sui et al., 2018) fabricated a one-dimensional metal-organic coordination material comprising Cd (II) and 4,4′-bipyridine-1,1′-bis-(styrene-3-carboxylate), featuring electron-deficient purple chromophores lining its channels. This material exhibits varying colors upon exposure to amine vapors. Although amine-sensitive chromophore-based methods offer fast and colorimetric detection approaches for biogenic amines, careful design and complex preparation processes are often required for these chromophores (Liu, He, et al., 2024).

The initial and oxidized states of the redox indicator show different colors, providing a noticeable distinction (Geng et al., 2023). The commonly used redox indicators for biogenic amine detection include 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) (Wang, Chen, et al., 2021), 2,2′-azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline)-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS), o-dianisidine (Qin et al., 2023), and so on. Typically, redox-based materials are coupled with enzyme reactions to quantify biogenic amines in food by measuring the color changes catalyzed by enzymes under varying conditions. TMB has become the most frequently used redox indicator for biogenic amine detection when combined with horseradish peroxidase to enable visual detection. Additionally, metal nanoparticles (Wang, Song, et al., 2020), metal oxide nanostructures (Xu, Mao, et al., 2021), and DNase (Wang, Tang, et al., 2024) with the same catalase activity can also be employed alongside TMB for biogenic amine detection purposes. In general terms, any reaction process involving hydrogen peroxide as an intermediate product can be used for indirect determination of biogenic amine content. Aside from TMB, ABTS and o-dianisidine serve as common reoxidation-based color-changing materials that can be used together with laccase to visually detect biogenic amines. Biological sources such as cota laccase found in Bacillus subtilis spores have gained attention due to their cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness and robust anti-interference characteristics in recent years (Qin et al., 2023). In a study by Qin et al. (Qin et al., 2023), a biosensor was developed utilizing a biogenic amines-induced pH elevation, which obstructs electron transfer between Cu ion sites on the spore surface of CotA-laccase, resulting in diminished catalytic oxidation activity towards chromogenic substrates.

Localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR)-based materials, such as metal nanoparticles, play an important role in the visual detection of biogenic amines. Compared with traditional organic dyes and other materials, they have higher extinction coefficients and can achieve nanomolar-level limits of detection (LOD) (Satija et al., 2016). For example, Huang et al. (Huang et al., 2017) developed a visual detection method based on electrostatic attraction to induce aggregation of Au NPs, bringing about color development with an impressively low LOD of 38 nM. Various factors affect the color change of nanoparticles during visual detection by influencing LSPR and subsequently impacting sensitivity. The size of metal nanoparticles is one such factor that affects the LSPR effect; larger particle sizes lead to redshifts in the LSPR peak. However, neither excessively large nor small particle sizes are optimal for histamine colorimetric detection (Rivera et al., 2024). At small sizes, the nonradiative absorption rules mostly and results from electron collision with the surface of the nanoparticle. On large distances, on which the damping rate rises with increasing nanoparticle size, the radiative scattering rules instead. Different detection outcomes arise from combining the effects of absorption and scattering of light. Additionally, the LSPR effect is influenced by the morphology of metal nanoparticles. The longitudinal band of gold nanorods can be finely tuned through their length/width ratio, resulting in enhanced detection sensitivity compared to spherical gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) (Rao et al., 2019). Furthermore, silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) demonstrate a lower wavelength and higher intensity plasmon resonance than Au NPs (Saadati et al., 2024). The surface plasmon resonance characteristics of Ag NPs can be tailored by modifying the thickness, edge length, and morphology of the nanoplate tip (Farshchi et al., 2023; Haber & Sokolov, 2017; Saadati et al., 2022). For example, silver nanoprisms exhibit distinct optical properties when compared to spherical silver nanoparticles (Nitinaivinij et al., 2014); they possess sharper and stronger peaks while also exhibiting heightened sensitivity towards changes in the refractive index of their surrounding environment (Amendola et al., 2010).

In addition to modifying the shape, size and composition of nanoparticles to manipulate their LSPR performance and subsequently affect the colorimetric effect, the use of chemical agents can also enhance the peak LSPR and improve the colorimetric response. For example, biogenic amines facilitate dithiocarbamate formation in the carbon disulfide/colloidal gold systems, leading to colloidal Au NPs aggregation that induces a redshift in the absorption spectrum of colloidal gold and consequent color changes (Choi et al., 2021). In addition, surface etching techniques can also modulate the LSPR peak shift of gold nanorods, which can be divided into three categories: (ⅰ) iodine-mediated etching (Zhong et al., 2019); (ⅱ) chromogenic substrate-based etching (Wang Na, et al., 2020); and (ⅲ) Fenton and Fenton-like reaction-mediated etching (Guo, Wang, et al., 2020). The colorimetric sensing technology based on surface etching has the unique advantages in providing multicolor responses. At present, most biogenic amine detection methods rely on the principle that nanoparticle aggregation affects the LPSR peak and alters the color of the solution, but this approach is plagued by issues of “pseudo-polymerization” and time consumption (Geng et al., 2023). In recent years, “non-aggregation” sensors mediated by controlled metal nanoparticle growth have been found to overcome the self-aggregation effects (Zhang, Wang, et al., 2018). These sensors can also be conveniently combined with test paper for fast detection, potentially becoming a novel material and trend in biogenic amine analysis.

Materials such as quantum dots exhibit fluoresce owing to their unique quantum confinement effect and have the advantages of tunable photoluminescence and excellent photostability (Li, Yang, et al., 2021). Currently, commonly used materials commonly include graphene quantum dots (Toloza et al., 2017), semiconductor quantum dots (Kim & Chang, 2016), carbon quantum dots (Shi et al., 2020), and their combinations (Yan et al., 2022). The optical effects produced by the quantum confinement effect can be adjusted by modifying the functional group, altering the size, or changing the solution environment (Toloza et al., 2017). Functional groups that have been recently applied in the detection of biogenic amines encompass amino groups (Toloza et al., 2017), N-acetyl-cysteine (Shi et al., 2020), thioglycolic acid (Khan et al., 2017), etc. For instance, Toloza et al. (Toloza et al., 2017) found that Fe3+ can quench the photoluminescence of amino-modified graphene quantum dots but not unmodified ones primarily because amino groups on both surfaces and edges of graphene quantum dots serve as coordination sites for metal ions which subsequently affect the photoluminescence. In addition, when the diameter of the quantum dot approaches and becomes smaller than the exciton Bohr radius, the motion of the exciton is limited by the dimension of the quantum dot. This results in an intermediate state for electron movement inside the dot, leading to a transformation from continuous energy levels to discrete energy levels and a significant manifestation of quantum confinement effect (Petryayeva et al., 2013). Khan et al. (Khan et al., 2017) synthesized thioglycolic acid-modified CdTe quantum dots for histamine detection. During optimization experiments, they found that as size decreased due to the quantum limiting effect, the energy of the conduction band increased, and the potential of the conduction band became more negative. Therefore, this enhanced electron donor capability towards ligands and improved photoluminescence properties were achieved (Lin, Wang, et al., 2019). Additionally, doping heteroatoms also has an effect on photoluminescence behavior. For example, the coordination between Eu3+ ions and carboxylic acid groups on graphene quantum dot surfaces induces aggregation, which subsequently leads to quenching of photoluminescence through processes like energy transfer or electron transfer (Bai et al., 2013). In the past few years, some research teams have successfully grafted quantum dots onto enriched materials such as covalent-organic frameworks (Zhang, Liu, et al., 2019) or incorporated them into composite structures with biorecognition components such as molecularly imprinted polymers (Kim & Chang, 2016) to enhance the sensitivity and specificity in detection.

In addition to the aforementioned common color-changing materials, variations in energy transfer or electron transfer resulting from combinations of multiple materials are also frequently observed in biogenic amine visualization sensors, giving rise to changes in color. For instance, Qi et al. (Qi et al., 2021) developed a fluorescence-responsive sensor for biogenic amines based on supramolecular host-guest interactions by exploiting the self-assembly property of phenazine derivatives and naphthalene diamide induced by hydrogen bonding. In this study, phenazine derivatives and naphthalene diamide, which has strong aggregation-induced emission effect, formed rod-like structures capable of emitting green fluorescence. The presence of biogenic amines disrupts intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions and π-π stacking, inducing charge transfer effects or photoluminescent hole transfer and altering the color of the fluorescent film. Guo et al. (Guo, Tian, et al., 2022) designed a fluorescent probe utilizing bimetallic Ln3+ (Eu3+ and Tb3+) complexes incorporated within nano-clay as a responsive element for histamine detection. In the presence of histamine, an energy transfer from Tb3+ to Eu3+ was induced, resulting in a significant decrease in Tb3+ emission intensity and a notable increase in Eu3+ emission intensity. This caused a color change of the probe from yellow to orange and eventually rose red. Remarkably, under optimal detection conditions, this probe exhibited an impressive LOD for histamine at 0.137 mg/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual sensors with different signal modes for detection of biogenic amine
ce:section.ce:para: Advanced materials are essential for designing biogenic amine visualization sensors and have gained great attention. Currently, in order to improve the sensitivity of detection and visual discrimination, an increasing number of researchers are combining multiple materials for the development of diverse visualization sensors. Herein, various types of visual sensors are discussed for detecting biogenic amines, with a focus on classifying their applications based on different signal modes (Table 1 ) and combine with other signal modes to form a multi-mode signal.

Colorimetric analysis has been widely recognized as a captivating method for on-site monitoring of biogenic amines because of its cost-effectiveness, user-friendly nature, facile miniaturization instrumentation, and direct visual readout capability (Qin et al., 2023). Currently, more and more biogenic amine assays in food are based on colorimetric analysis. Wang et al. (Wang, Zhao, et al., 2023) developed a bioamine-responsive colorimetric film by incorporating bagasse cellulose and cellulose nanofibers with sugarcane peel anthocyanins (Fig. 2 A). Upon exposure to ammonia vapors, the colorimetric film presented remarkable color change (ΔE>5) and rapid response within 1–2 s. Nevertheless, the diffusion process of amine gas through the film was relatively slow, and the closed design limited the contact between amine gas and anthocyanins, thus affecting the accuracy and sensitivity of the film's performance (Wu, Liu, et al., 2024; Zheng et al., 2022). Recently, Huang's group (Huang, Wang, et al., 2024) introduced an innovative approach by utilizing MIL-100(Fe) as a functional filler in combination with anthocyanins/pectin for monitoring amine gas levels. Compared to previous research, this is the first to use MIL-100 (Fe) as a functional filler to improve the amine sensitivity of smart films used to monitor the freshness of frozen meat and investigate its mechanism of action. In this study, they found that the adsorption capacity of the film was enhanced by MIL-100(Fe) through chemisorption and intraparticle diffusion mechanisms, thereby improving sensitivity towards volatile biogenic amines. However, the majority of indicator labels can only interact with volatile substances present in the packaging headspace, resulting in delayed information acquisition. In a study conducted by Wang et al. (Wang, Chen, et al., 2023), a pH-responsive colorimetric microneedle patch was developed using hydrogel-based microneedles obtained from silk fibroin meth acryloyl and bromothymol blue for the detection of biogenic amines in meat (Fig. 2B). The result shows the correlation between the RGB values calculated from color pictures of microneedles patches and TVB-N value of salmon samples, as well as a linear dependence model and good correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.975). These arrays facilitate the transportation of internal food fluids to the backing layer and enable biomolecule extraction from salmon within 8 min, thus offering real-time, fast, and accurate information. Despite their ability to intuitively and quickly detect biogenic amines in food samples, these methods only provide semi-quantitative and qualitative data.

Except for pigments, other non-nanomaterials such as organic dyes, polymers, and redox indicators are commonly employed for the colorimetric detection of biogenic amines. Lyu et al. (Lv et al., 2019) proposed a colorimetric method based on azo reagent selected as the indicator due to its characteristic spectrometry when reacting with histamine solution. The colors of homemade colorimetric sensing units changed slightly from yellow to orange-red and pink as the concentration of histamine varied from low to high. The gray values of units were chosen for statistical and quantitative analysis. The relationship between gray values and the histamine content showed a high correlation (R2 = 0.9926), with the LOD value of histamine was below 20 μg/mL. Sudalaimani et al. (Sudalaimani et al., 2020) developed a ninhydrin-based method for detecting putrescine and cadaverine in sardines and tilapia, achieving the LODs of 10 ppm and 40 ppm, respectively. Jang et al. (Jang et al., 2023), on the other hand, prepared a polydiacetylene-based hydrogel magnetic bead sensor for the sensitive detection of biogenic amines (Fig. 2C). The sensor is mainly composed of alginate and polyethylene glycol liposome. Alginate imparts a three-dimensional porous structure to the hydrogel magnetic beads, providing a large surface area for adsorbing alkaline biogenic amines in abundance. Upon stimulation, the conjugated skeleton of polydiacetylene liposomes undergoes conformational changes, resulting in a color transition from blue to red. Amine oxidase exhibits specific recognition towards biogenic amines, generating hydrogen peroxide as a byproduct. With the assistance of peroxidase, this reaction induces color change in the indicator through redox reactions. For example, Yao et al. (Yao et al., 2022) developed a colorimetric method based on diamine oxidase that enables H2O2 production upon detection of histamine and putrescine in milk. The LODs of histamine and putrescine are 0.652 2 and 1.134 1 μM, respectively.

In recent years, nanomaterials have gained popularity as a material of choice for designing biogenic amine sensors due to their unique surface effects, small size effects, quantum size effects, and quantum confinement effects. Among these materials, metal nanoparticles such as Au NPs are commonly utilized for biogenic amine detection. For example, Li et al. (Li, Gan, et al., 2021) developed a detection system for biogenic amines using polyethylene glycol-modified Au NPs and dopamine (Fig. 2D). The presence of biogenic amines in the sample would trigger rapid dopamine polymerization within the detection system, leading to the formation of a polydopamine layer on the surface of Au NPs and causing a redshift in the LSPR peak. The color change from wine red to black was observed in this detection system with a LOD at 2.8 μg/mL within the concentration range of 1–100 μg/mL. To enhance specificity and visual recognition in biogenic amine detection, Xu et al. (Xu, Wu, et al., 2021) proposed an innovative multicolor histamine sensor based on diamine oxidase-specific catalysis and iodine-mediated gold nanorods (Au NRs) etching (Fig. 2E). In this study, histamine was specifically recognized by diamine oxidase, leading to hydrogen peroxide production. Assisted by MoO4 2−, hydrogen peroxide quickly oxidized iodide to iodine, bringing about the longitudinal corrosion of Au NRs and consequent blue shift in LSPR peak, resulting in distinct color changes in the solution. The method has a good detection limit of approximately 11.39 μM. Although these methods offer easy and intuitive identification and quantification of histamine, they typically involve multiple steps and may suffer from decomposition of intermediate H2O2 during the two-step enzymic reaction, thereby prolonging detection time and reducing sensitivity. Consequently, there is an urgent need for developing a sensor that improves the usage of intermediate H2O2 in the enzyme cascade reactions. In Liu et al.’s study (Liu, Gan, et al., 2024), they proposed a sensor comprising TMB and hydrogen peroxide @ diamine oxidase hybrid microsphere prepared via biomineralization method. Compared to previous studies, this approach enabled one-step detection of histamine through dual-enzyme cascade reaction and improves the usage of intermediate H2O2 in the enzyme cascade reaction with a LOD as low as 0.15 μg/mL, surpassing that achieved by free enzyme detection systems. Furthermore, the method exhibited a reduced instrumental analysis duration of 5 min.

Fluorescence mode is a commonly used signal mode in visual detection due to its high sensitivity, fast response, minimized background signals, and ease of automation (Wei et al., 2023). In a study conducted by Hasanoval et al. (Hasanova et al., 2022), they developed a fluorometric sensor based on dithioerythritol functionalized Au NPs for detecting biogenic amines. The principle underlying this study primarily relies on the reaction between phthalaldehyde reagent and biogenic amine on the surface of Au NPs functionalized with 1,4-dithioerythritol under alkaline conditions, resulting in the generation of 1-alkylthio-2-alkylindole and subsequent fluorescence emission. Within this detection system, the LOD for biogenic amines was determined to be within the range of 4.20–27.1 nM with an analysis time of 2 min. Yang et al. firstly developed a fluorescent probe HDXM that can simultaneously detect SO2 derivatives (HSO3 −/SO3 2−) and biogenic amines (Yang, Yan, et al., 2024). The HDXM fluorescent probe utilizes benzopyran derivatives as fluorescent groups, which are connected to the electron-withdrawing group 2,2-dimethyl-1,3-dioxane-4,6-dione through a C=C bond. In this work, the HDXM fluorescent probe can achieve ultrafast response (within 3s) to HSO3 −/SO3 2− and biogenic amines, with LODs of 1.02 and 6.89 μM, respectively. Fluorescent change caused by HSO3 − or biogenic amines is occurring photo-induced electron transfer or enhancing internal charge transfer process. Compared with other fluorescent probes, this multifunctional probe can be used as a versatile fluorescent material in various applications, including flexible films, glass coatings, impregnating dyes, printing, and fingerprint ink. This sensor can reduce the overall workload, lower costs, and eliminate the need to spend time exploring testing conditions. In addition to detecting multiple substances, Cao et al. utilized N-doped green-emitting carbon dots (N-GCDs) and bovine serum albumin-gold nano-clusters (BSA-AuNCs) as fluorescent probes to achieve dual responses of temperature and volatile biogenic amines (Cao et al., 2024). In this work, the author loaded N-GCDs with a solvent chromotropic effect and pH-sensitive BSA-AuNCs into polyvinyl alcohol-sodium alginate hydrogel to obtain fluorescent hydrogel and then put them together with water (H2O)/dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) mixture into a lunch box containing fish meat. When the ambient temperature was higher than the threshold value, the H2O/DMSO mixture in solid state was destroyed, and the fluorescence of N-GCDs was quenched by melting into the gel to achieve temperature detection. As the temperature rises even more, the amount of volatile biogenic amines increases. This changes the pH of the environment and the brightness of the BSA-AuNCs, which makes it possible to see biogenic amines.

Ratiometric fluorescent sensing systems show great potential for enhanced detection accuracy through self-calibration of the signal intensities from two signal units, with one unit serving as an internal reference to normalize analyte indicators (Chen et al., 2022). Liu et al. (Liu, Jiang, et al., 2022) developed a ratiometric fluorescent label based on zein film modified with rhodamine B and fluorescein (Fig. 3 A). Among these components, fluorescein emitted green fluorescence as a responsive signal, which could be quenched under alkaline conditions, while rhodamine B emitted red fluorescence acting as a reference signal. The designed intelligent label was employed for accurate real-time monitoring of food freshness in situ and visually.

In addition to ratiometric fluorescence sensors that can enhance detection accuracy, the incorporation of biometric elements (such as peptides, antibodies, aptamers, and enzymes) in sensor design can effectively augment detection precision. Shi et al.’s study (Shi et al., 2020) utilized high-affinity peptides and carbon quantum dots to develop a fluorescent biosensor for detecting biogenic amines in canned fish. This fluorescence biosensor for histamine using newly designed peptides through biopanning approaches. The interaction between carbon quantum dots and high-affinity peptide segments led to efficient quenching of their fluorescence through electron transfer interactions. On the contrary, fluorescence was recovered due to stronger peptide-target interactions in the presence of histamine (Fig. 3B). Based on this principle, fluorescence intensity was measured for quantifying histamine, and the LOD for histamine was determined to be 13 ppb. To further enhance detection sensitivity, researchers explored the enrichment of biogenic amines from the samples. The fluorescent sensor proposed by Mao et al. (Mao et al., 2019) utilized a nanoporous alumina membrane modified with carbon dots and Fe3O4@Au magnet nanocomposites (Fig. 3C). When the Fe3O4@Au magnet nanocomposites captured histamine were brought close to the carbon dots on the nanoporous membrane, a fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect occurred, which quenched the fluorescence of the carbon dots. This method could determine histamine concentration with a LOD of 70 pM. Recently, it has been discovered that using lanthanide element donors with long excited state lifetimes (up to milliseconds) in fluorescence resonance energy transfer allows for time-gated detection, which effectively reduces sample spontaneous fluorescence and other interference signals, resulting in highly sensitive and consistent analysis (Algar et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2022). In the study conducted by Fu et al. (Fu et al., 2022), they investigated the time-gated Förster resonance energy transfer process in the fluorescent competitive assays, which involved different terbium-donor (antibody/aptamer) and various dyes acceptors (SufloCya5.5/ATTO655/Cya5.5) (Fig. 3D). Remarkably, Cya5.5 exhibited minimal aggregation and exceptional sensitivity compared to other dyes. Furthermore, their findings revealed that aptamer-based sensors demonstrated heightened sensitivity in detecting low concentrations of histamine due to the compact size of the complex formed between the aptamer and complementary chain, as well as the close proximity between receptor and donor molecules. The antibody-based assay achieved a remarkable LOD of 0.03 μg/mL, while the aptamer-based assay yielded an equally impressive LOD of 0.19 μg/mL. Compared to the traditional ELISA-based detection method for histamine (∼150 min), the proposed detection system in this work only needs 120 min (IgG-based assays) or 80 min (aptamer-based assays) and measuring for 5 s.

Chemiluminescent sensors are developed based on the emission of light (ultraviolet, visible, or infrared), which is created due to the chemical reactions (Danchuk et al., 2020). Sensors based on chemiluminescence offer advantages such as rapidity, ease of operation, simplicity in procedure, a wide linear range, low LOD, and no necessity for a mono-chromator (Ahangari et al., 2021). Gou et al. (Gou et al., 2022) prepared pH-responsive hybrid materials with dynamic photoluminescence by leveraging the alkaline characteristics of biogenic amines to detect these compounds in food (Fig. 4 A). In this study, a lanthanide-doped pH-responsive luminescent sensor (montmorillonite@[Eu(Phen)2]-pefloxacin) was synthesized to achieve reversible multicolor luminescence conversion (from blue to red) through pH modulation in the solution. This development led to the creation of a portable test strip for detecting biogenic amines. The LOD for ammonia was determined to be 1.9 mM. These results indicate that the portable test strip can serve as an effective and rapid food spoilage sensor for detecting vapors emitted by biogenic amines. Moreover, a one-to-two decoder was designed based on the fluorescence response of montmorillonite@[Eu(Phen)2]-pefloxacin to pH change in this work. The constructed logic circuit had broad application prospects in computer-aided environmental pH monitoring. Beyond that, hydrogen peroxide produced as a result of the reaction between biogenic amine and oxidase, can generate optical phenomena when combined with luminol reagent (Liu, Gan, et al., 2024). In this context, Omanovic-Miklicanin and Valzacchi (Omanovic-Miklicanin & Valzacchi, 2017) fabricated two chemiluminescent sensors derived from putrescine oxidase and diamine oxidase respectively for detecting putrescine in fish and other meat products. The LOD for the two sensors was 0.8 mg/L and 1.3 mg/L, respectively. This sensor enables rapid and sensitive detection of putrescine in food through color changes, demonstrating excellent specificity. In addition to hydrogen peroxide, researchers have also explored the use of nanoparticles with peroxidase activity such as Ag NPs and Au NPs in chemiluminescent-based sensors for biogenic amine detection (Kuo et al., 2018). The luminol chemiluminescence reaction can be catalyzed by enzymes or transition metal cations, either free or complexed with organic or inorganic ligands. Santafé et al. (Santafé et al., 2010) proposed a novel method for histamine detection based on the use of a chelating lipid Langmuir-Blodgett film as a nanometer-thick immobilization matrix and employing luminol as the chemiluminescence reagent. The formation of multiple chelation complexes between the lipid and histamine prevents the metal cation from catalyzing the luminol chemiluminescence reaction, leading to a decrease in emitted light signal intensity. This approach achieves a low LOD of 2 μg/mL for histamine concentration determination.

Except for the chemiluminescence activation mode, there has been clear evidence of histamine-induced chemiluminescence inactivation in recent years. Wang et al. (Wang, Liu, et al., 2014) synthesized Mg-Al-CO3 layered double hydroxide that acts as a chemiluminescence catalyst and a resonance energy transfer acceptor. This compound enhances the strength of the bis (2,4,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate-H2O2 system's chemiluminescence (Fig. 4B). However, the presence of biogenic amines significantly suppresses the intensity of chemiluminescence. The switching behavior of chemiluminescence was attributed to the displacement of O-H⋯O bonding (between formamide and layered double hydroxide nanosheets) by O-H⋯N bonding (between biogenic amines and layered double hydroxide nanosheets). Consequently, this system was used for actual sample detection and successfully determined the histamine content in samples. The LOD for Mg-Al-CO3 layered double hydroxide towards histamine was found to be 3.2 nM.

Recently, a series of dual-mode sensing platforms have been developed using two or more response mechanisms with relatively independent signal transduction. In comparison to the single-mode sensors, the results provided by these dual-mode sensors can be verified mutually and self-corrected, thereby improving detection accuracy and reliability (Li, Dong, et al., 2023). Bi et al. (Bi et al., 2020) fabricated an Au NPs-based sensor capable of colorimetry and fluorescence monitoring of biogenic amines. In their study, the formation of histamine-Au NPs network resulted in enhanced surface plasmon resonance and n-π∗ conjugation, leading to a color change from red to blue in the system. Meanwhile, the large amino groups in histamine-Au NPs exhibited higher molecular orbital properties and enabled turn-on fluorescence mode. The LOD for the ultraviolet–visible spectrum assay and fluorescence spectrum assay were determined as 0.87 nM and 2.04 nM, respectively. Chen's group (Chen et al., 2022) introduced a novel material, namely 4-(dimethylamino)styryl)quinoxalin-2(1H)-one (ASQ), which possesses a typical donor (D)-acceptor (A) structure with nonmonotonic color and fluorescence responses upon pH stimulation through protonation/deprotonation on D/A moieties (Fig. 5 A). In this study, ASQ demonstrated an LOD towards biogenic amine at 5.35 × 10−7 M. Zhong et al. synthesized a simple benzopyran-based fluorescent probe DCA-Apa for the detection of biogenic amines. The detection principle was mainly based on the fact that basic amines could hydrolyze the ester groups in DCA-Apa to form the compound of DCA-Ap phenoxy anion (Zhong, Zhao, et al., 2024). DCA-Apa offers ultra-fast response, near-infrared emission, and a significant Stokes shift, enabling dual-channel detection of biogenic amines through colorimetric (blue to light yellow) and fluorescent (light pink to orange) methods, with a low limit of detection of 9.03 μM for diethylamine. The design of highly selective and sensitive probes remains a critical challenge because of the similar chemical properties and basicity shared by biogenic amines and other amines. In recent years, reaction-based fluorescent probes have attracted considerable attention due to their exceptional selectivity and sensitivity. Recently, Wang et al. (Wang, Xin, et al., 2021) designed a pyrrolopyrrole cyanine as a reaction-based fluorescent probe with distinct colorimetric and ratiometric dual-channel capabilities. The underlying mechanism involves synergistic aza-Michael addition and B-N detachment reactions, followed by hydrolysis to produce low-conjugated diketopyrrolopyrrole and heteroaromatic acetonitrile compounds. This fluorescent probe displayed excellent selectivity and sensitivity, with a LOD for biogenic amine at 62.1 nM. Moreover, the incorporation of biomolecular components such as aptamers can further improve the sensitivity and specificity of the detection. In their study, Wang et al. (Wang, Yang, et al., 2022) proposed a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode specific method for the detection of histamine in fish based on aptamer recognition combined with time-resolved fluorescence resonance energy transfer (Fig. 5B). In this study, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer phenomenon is caused by the proximity of Au NPs and NaYF4:Ce/Tb, resulting in the quenching of the fluorescence signal in the detection system. The presence of histamine competes with Au NPs to capture the aptamer and release it from the AuNP surface, inducing fluorescence recovery. Under optimal conditions, the LOD for histamine was 4.57 nM. A highly sensitive, rapid, and accurate dual-mode detection method for analyzing histamine content in food was established through mutual verification and combined use of the two modes, thereby offering a reference for monitoring food freshness.

Aside from the multimodal vision, integrating vision with other modalities such as olfactory and thermal sensing holds important practical implications for enhancing detection accuracy and expanding application domains. In this context, Wang et al. (Wang, Ding, et al., 2024) proposed a three-in-one sensor, 3,6-bis(dimethylamino)-9-(ethylthio)xanthylium, capable of detecting biogenic amines through multimodal responses, including olfactory perception, colorimetric changes, and fluorescence signals (Fig. 5C). This study showed distinct fluorescent and colorimetric responses towards biogenic amines with an improved LOD (0.03 μM), attributed to the nucleophilic substitution reaction of amines. In addition, in conjunction with ethanethiol as an olfactory alerting indicator, biogenic amines can be easily identified without requiring additional equipment or instruments, thus facilitating convenient real-time detection. To enhance detection specificity further, Yang et al. (Yang et al., 2023) proposed a new multi-mode immunoassay based on Fe3O4@Au NPs and antibodies for biogenic amine detection (Fig. 5D). This approach combines the advanced magnetic-thermal properties of Fe3O4 particles with the colorimetric effect of Au NPs, enabling multi-signal readout. This sensor provides a convenient quantitative platform for the detection of histamine and putrescine, achieving a LOD as low as 0.31 and 0.17 ng/mL in magnetic mode, 4.39 and 2.31 ng/mL in photothermal mode, as well as 10 and 10 ng/mL in naked eye mode, respectively. The integration of colorimetric-thermal-magnetic three-in-one detection ensures sensitive and reliable results, which are vital for real sample analysis, thereby expanding the application scope of lateral flow detection in food safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latest applications of smartphone-based smart sensing platform for biogenic amine visual detection
ce:section.ce:para: Visual sensing technology, as a commonly used detection method, has demonstrated commendable performance in ensuring food safety. However, with the increasing complexity of safety issues related to food, there is a growing demand for testing methods that offer timeliness, convenience, and accuracy (Ling et al., 2023). Moreover, one significant challenge in the development of visual sensors lies in achieving intelligentization (Huang et al., 2018). Smartphones have emerged as intelligent devices with diverse applications in the fields of detection due to their portability, cost-effectiveness, user-friendly operation, and strong computing power. By enabling colorimetric sensing detection outside laboratory settings, smartphones facilitate instant and on-site testing needs. Therefore, leveraging smartphones can comprehensively improve the performance of sensor (Geng et al., 2023).

The accuracy of visual detection is heavily influenced by the efficacy of color signals (Geng et al., 2023). Given the inherent limitations in the naked eye observations and variations in color sensitivity among inspectors, it becomes imperative to gather color parameters from digital images for data analysis purposes. Color spaces provide a mathematical framework for representing colors, converting complex color information into measurable and manipulable formats (Mazur et al., 2024). Commonly employed color parameters for biogenic amine detection encompass red-green-blue (RGB) (Lee et al., 2019), gray value (Li, Ren, et al., 2020), hue-saturation-value (HSV) (Lu et al., 2023), commission internationale De L’ eclairage (CIE) (Wang, Huang, et al., 2022), among others.

The RGB value is the fundamental and widely employed color parameter in the visual detection of biogenic amines, which are divided into three distinct color channels: R (red), G (green), and B (blue). These values can be obtained by analyzing digital photos using smartphone applications like the RGB color picker or software tools such as ColorSchemer Studio and ImageJ. While extracting and analyzing RGB values from digital images, it is possible to establish a standard curve relating either single channel values or ratios between different channels with the concentration of biogenic amines during analysis. For instance, Qin et al. (Qin et al., 2023) quantified biogenic amine levels in raw meat samples by incorporating ΔB values acquired from the RGB analysis application. Ye et al. (Ye et al., 2022) used the B/R ratio as a chromatic signal to quantify biogenic amines in meat samples. Luo et al. (Luo et al., 2021) and Wei et al. (Wei, Huang, et al., 2024), respectively, quantified biogenic amines utilizing (R + G + B)/B and G/B values. In addition to RGB values, gray values are commonly employed in color analysis due to their lower computational complexity; however, they are not suitable for multi-color systems (Geng et al., 2023). HSV is another widely used color parameter that offers higher sensitivity compared to RGB. It consists of three channels: Hue, Saturation, and Value (Su et al., 2015). The Saturation channel reduces interference from ambient light (Coleman et al., 2019), whereas the Hue channel exhibits sensitivity towards refractive index variations and performs better in colorimetric analyses based on the LSPR effect of nanoparticles (Reinhard et al., 2020). The CIE values are less frequently applied but can be categorized into two color spaces: CIEXYZ and CIELab. The latter is predominantly utilized for visual detection of biogenic amines, as it closely aligns with human visual perception (Maimaiti et al., 2024; Wang, Huang, et al., 2022). The limitations associated with RGB values in quantitative analysis are effectively addressed by this color space (Shen et al., 2012), while compensating for deficiencies inherent in other color spaces (Starešinič et al., 2011). All three-color parameters (gray value, HSV, and CIE) can be converted from RGB values. Nevertheless, direct conversion between RGB and CIELab is not possible; the RGB value must first be converted to CIEXYZ value, and then further transformed into CIELab value (León et al., 2006).

The establishment of a standard curve correlating color values with biogenic amine concentrations is an essential step in the analysis of biogenic amines in samples. Nevertheless, the process of establishing such a standard curve has certain limitations, including the constant adjustment required for analyte concentration and the need to select the most suitable color channel (Geng et al., 2023). High-resolution devices have been employed to capture color parameters from different samples, which are and then analyzed by algorithms to determine the content of biogenic amines. This research area has gained significant popularity in recent years. In the field of biogenic amine colorimetric sensing, commonly used algorithms include partial least squares regression (PLSR) (Huang, Zou, et al., 2014), principal component analysis (PCA) (Lee et al., 2019), linear discriminant analysis (LDA) (Du, Lao, et al., 2022), hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) (Zhong, Huo, et al., 2018), you only look once (YOLO) (Lu et al., 2023), support vector machine (SVM) (Taheri-Garavand et al., 2019), artificial neural network (ANN) (Sun et al., 2022), convolutional neural network (CNN) (Tan et al., 2022), and deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) (Guo, Wang, et al., 2020), etc. Among them, traditional algorithms such as PLSR, PCA and LDA require manual extraction of image features, which are then inputted into a classifier for classification and prediction. Typically, this procedure relies heavily on human involvement and specific domain expertise. In addition, the high cross-reactivity of colorimetric sensing arrays enables the generation of non-linear multidimensional datasets (Guo, Wang, et al., 2020), which cannot be accurately extracted and analyzed, resulting in subpar prediction performance. In recent years, deep learning techniques represented by ANN and CNN have demonstrated their efficacy in capturing complex relationships in non-linear data with superior prediction and classification performance (Zhou et al., 2019). Furthermore, deep learning autonomously acquires representative features from the original data throughout the training process. This diminishes reliance on manual feature engineering. Consequently, significant advancements have been made in the field of biogenic amine detection. For instance, Mohammadi Lalabadi et al. (Mohammadi Lalabadi et al., 2020) conducted an analysis comparing the accuracy of SVM and ANN; their findings indicated that ANN outperformed SVM in terms of accuracy. While SVM is a non-parametric model, ANN is a parametric model characterized by constant size. By training the network model with a substantial amount of data, it can autonomously acquire the essential image features, so as to achieve high recognition accuracy (Lecun et al., 2015). Additionally, they found that the recognition accuracy of the same algorithm varies across different regions of the fish, mainly due to the different gradient changes in coloration. Consequently, choosing the most important discriminative region during algorithm design is imperative for enhancing the accuracy.

DCNN and its derivative algorithms exhibit promising potential as recognition algorithms for nonlinear multidimensional data analysis, such as colorimetric sensing arrays (Gurovich et al., 2019; Wang, Yan, et al., 2020). They have shown superiority in the field of food quality detection. Guo and his group (Guo, Wang, et al., 2020) developed a freshness prediction platform that mimics olfaction to monitor biogenic amines in meat. This platform utilizes cross-reaction colorimetric barcode combination technology to monitor biogenic amine gases and provide odor fingerprints. Furthermore, the DCNN is employed to process and identify these odor fingerprints, giving rise to a prediction platform with high accuracy. The predictive platform presented in this study can provide both odor fingerprints and fingerprint recognition functions, but the DCNN can only output visual signals for quantifying biogenic amines. Ensemble learning constitutes an efficacious method for tackling this problem. Ensemble deep learning models integrate various deep learning architectures within a cohesive framework to address intricate tasks. Furthermore, enhancing the model's performance and generalization can be achieved by augmenting the size and diversity of the dataset (Qiu, Chen, et al., 2025). Recently, a dual mode-based visual sensor consisted of a hydrogen-bonded organic framework (Eu@HOF-12) and blueberry anthocyanins was prepared by Maimaiti et al. (Maimaiti et al., 2024). In this work, the mechanism for the luminescence quenching of Eu@HOF-12 by biogenic amines is a-photo-induced electron transfer. The LODs of Eu@HOF-12 for putrescine, cadaverine, spermine, histamine, and tyramine are as low as 5.3, 3.4, 2.5, 2.3, and 1.8 μM, respectively. Unlike previous studies, this study integrates and transforms visual and olfactory information using a back propagation neural network (BPNN) approach. The mean squared error of BPNN1 (visual) and BPNN2 (olfactory) is less than 3.8 × 10−7, indicating that their identification accuracy is significantly higher than 0.999. These integrated models aim to combine the benefits of various models in order to improve the overall performance, stability, and robustness of the final prediction model. Although deep learning is superior to traditional methods, it lacks interpretability and explanatory analysis in model design, making it difficult to understand the decision-making process of the model. Lin et al. (Lin, Ma et al., 2024) used t-distributed stochastic embedding visualization methods to explore the mathematical interpretability of features automatically extracted by CNN architectures and provide insights into underlying patterns and data structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latest advancements of smartphone-based smart detection devices
ce:section.ce:para: The combination of smartphones and visual technology offers a wider range of applications for visual sensing. Within seconds, photos captured by smartphones can provide digital color resolution parameters, allowing a qualitative assessment of food quality based on visual cues (González-Ceballos et al., 2020). This overcomes the limitations associated with traditional detection methods that rely on subjective human perception and inaccurate colorimetric correlation detection (Luo et al., 2021). Smartphones play multiple roles in biogenic amine detection, encompassing signal detection, data processing, data transmission, and display capabilities, among others. In most instances, these functions are combined to achieve multiple objectives.

Wei et al. (Wei, Huang, et al., 2024) employed Mn3O4 possessing laccase activity in conjunction with a smartphone for the quantification of spermine in food samples. In this study, the researchers transferred the reaction solution containing Mn3O4 into a 96-well plate, captured an image using a smartphone under well-lit conditions, extracted RGB values from the image utilizing Chrominance Detector software, and subsequently established a standard curve correlating detected substances with RGB values to determine spermine content in different food samples. The proposed method exhibits outstanding sensitivity and selectivity, with LOD as low as 0.094 μg/mL. In order to facilitate on-site and real-time detection, Li et al. (Li, Ren, et al., 2020) combined amine-sensitive polydiacetylene vesicles with test strips to establish a colorimetric test strip which was then analyzed for grayscale intensity using ImageJ and ‘Color Grab’ software installed on a smartphone (Fig. 6 A). The colorimetric assay strip exhibited excellent stability and sensitivity, with histamine detection limit set at 70 ppm. In addition, Li et al. (Li, Jiang, et al., 2024), constructed a BCN probe that could achieve both colorimetric and fluorescent response for the detection of biogenic amines. The authors loaded the BCN probe onto filter paper to create sensing labels, then combined it with smartphones to create a visual detection platform. In this study, the smartphone was able to recognize the RGB value of the label in both daylight and ultraviolet light. The relationship between the RGB value and the TVB-N value was established using (R + B)/(R + B + G) and G/R as the longitudinal coordinates, respectively, and the correlation coefficient was greater than 0.99. The BCN probe, as a dye, can be processed into film, dyeing, printing, and coating, and it has the potential to monitor meat freshness in real time.

Although the aforementioned method is fast and sensitive, food products emit a diverse range of biogenic amine gases during the deterioration process, each possessing distinct characteristics. Consequently, single gas detection can be easily confounded by non-target gases. In addition, achieving high-throughput analysis of samples remains a formidable challenge. To this end, more and more researchers are currently developing colorimetric sensor arrays and combining artificial intelligence to attain heightened sensitivity detection and expedited sample analysis. Guo et al. (Guo, Wang, et al., 2020) developed a user-friendly platform for swift barcode scanning and real-time identification of biogenic amine content via smartphones and DCNN (Fig. 6B). In their study, the authors initially fabricated colorimetric barcodes comprising 20 different types of chitosan, dye, and cellulose acetate nanocomposites for the detection of different biogenic amines in food samples. The LOD for biogenic amines were below 50 ppb. Subsequently, these barcodes were learned, recognized and processed using DCNN to form a comprehensive dataset integrated into a smartphone application interface. This enabled the identification and quantification of biogenic amines by scanning the colorimetric barcodes from different samples using the smartphone app with an impressive overall prediction accuracy of 98.5 %. The utilization of microfluidic paper-based analytical device has several advantages, such as low reagent consumption, simple operation, short detection time, and portability. These advantages make them highly attractive for developing colorimetric microfluidic paper-based analytical devices integrated with smartphones. Liu et al. (Liu, Zhang, et al., 2024) proposed a CNN combined with a microfluidic paper-based assisted smartphone for the accurate detection of biogenic amines in pork (Fig. 6C). The study utilized the color reaction principle of bromocresol green with biogenic amines to create a specific colorimetric microfluidic paper base. Subsequently, the color images of the microfluidic paper base were captured using a smartphone and trained using the ResNet50 classification model, achieving an impressive accuracy rate of 97.1 %. In addition, they developed a customized Android application for biogenic amine detection on smartphones, enabling convenient on-site and real-time testing. The estimated cost of materials was approximately 0.16 $US/device, and the entire process could be completed in less than 5 min. Hydrogel-based microneedle patches have also been identified as effective tools for rapid extraction of target analytes from samples, offering fast and accurate real-time information. Therefore, these patches are increasingly being adopted as reliable platforms for detection. Wang et al. (Wang, Xia, et al., 2024) developed a colorimetric microneedle sensor for biogenic amine detection using deep learning-assisted smartphone technology (Fig. 6D). The sensor, containing anthocyanin, penetrates meat and extracts tissue fluid through capillary action. When biogenic amines were present, the change in tissue fluid pH causes a corresponding change in the color of the microneedle sensor. The CNN-trained images achieved prediction accuracy exceeding 95.3 %, which was then integrated into an Android software on smartphones to transmit the captured images to servers for subsequent display of results on the screens.

The colorimetric measurement process can be interfered by ambient light, and methods such as external light sources can be employed to eliminate detection errors caused by variations in natural light (Yang et al., 2021; Fu, Xie, et al., 2020). Ye and his colleagues (Ye et al., 2022) utilized a homemade photosensitive colorimeter for accurate monitoring of biogenic amines in meat (Fig. 6E). The observed color change primarily depends on the oxidation of TMB by singlet oxygen generated from 2′, 4′, 5′, 7' -iodofluorescein. In the presence of biogenic amines, the pH value of the system increases, leading to a deceleration in the oxidation rate of TMB. As a result, the system gradually shows the color of 2′, 4′, 5′, 7′-iodofluorescein. In order to facilitate on-site detection and minimize smartphone-related detection errors, they developed a device comprising a camera obscura equipped with LED lights. These LED lights can be activated to assist in image acquisition while ensuring that the exterior surface of the camera obscura is blackened to prevent interference from surrounding environmental lighting. The whole analytical process took about 5 min, and a good linear relationship between the response signal and the trimethylamine concentrations (R2 = 0.985).

With the development of artificial intelligence, deep learning has great advantages in improving detection accuracy and other related aspects. Biogenic amines are common biomarkers produced by pathogens, and their color reaction is extremely important for pathogen identification and quantification. For example, Yang et al. (Yang, Liu, et al., 2021) proposed a pathogen identification system based on machine learning combined with paper color array. The paper color array is made up of a paper substrate impregnated with 23 different chromophore dyes and dye combinations, each of which can react with volatile organic compounds produced by at least one type of microorganism. These dyes change color when exposed to reactive organic compounds emitted by the pathogen of interest, such as bromophenol blue, which can detect alkaline biogenic amines produced by Escherichia coli O157: H7. These color changes are digitized and used to train multi-layer neural networks that can accurately recognize and quantify pathogens. The multi-layer neural network architecture used in this work has the advantage of providing highly automated and repeatable solutions while also being known for its noise tolerance. The trained paper chromogenic array multi-layer neural network system can distinguish between Escherichia coli and other live pathogens, and it can detect Escherichia coli O157:H7 and Listeria monocytogenes on lettuce with a 91–95 % accuracy. This system is designed to make it easier to detect and identify non-destructive food pathogens by eliminating the need for enrichment, cultivation, incubation, and other sample preparation steps. Furthermore, deep learning exhibits immense potential for analyzing large volumes of samples containing different structural analogues. Recently, Zhong et al. (Zhong, Xin, et al., 2024) have constructed a colorimetric sensor array using three MnO2-based nanozymes with oxidase-like activity to differentiate between three mixtures of unsaturated fatty acids in varying proportions and combinations. They employed the “image segmentation - feature extraction” deep learning (ISFE-DL) technique to segment the sensing elements of the color sensor array individually, followed by feature extraction from the single-hole images. The data were then trained by MobileNetV3-small model and embedded in a smartphone for simultaneous qualitative and quantitative analysis of different unsaturated fatty acids. Compared with traditional methods, the ISFE-DL method can automatically extract image features and then segment them, providing more feature values and increasing the sample size in limited tests, thus enabling efficient detection of compounds with similar structures. These aforementioned deep learning algorithms play a crucial role in enhancing the capabilities of visual sensor detection, while future integration with innovative biogenic amine visual sensors could further improve the detection efficiency and expand application possibilities.

Many conventional techniques, including HPLC, IEC, CE, etc., have been extensively used for the detection of biogenic amines in food. Nevertheless, due to the escalating number and complexity of food safety concerns, the timeliness, convenience and operability of the detection methods assume paramount importance. Nonetheless, the above methods suffer from drawbacks involving expensive equipment requirements, time-consuming procedures and dependence on professional technicians, which fail to meet the current needs of detection. In recent years, the visual sensors have gained widespread popularity for biogenic amine detection owing to their excellent sensitivity, affordability, and user-friendly application (rapidity, directness and real-time capability, etc.). In this review, we first provide an overview of commonly used materials in the design of visual sensors along with analysis of their respective advantages and disadvantages. Subsequently, employing the response modes as a classification criterion, we summarize the latest research progress pertaining to existing sensors based on colorimetric, fluorescence, chemiluminescence modes as well as combinations thereof; additionally analyzing the strengths challenges and solutions of the sensors utilizing different modes. Finally, we present a comprehensive summary encompassing color space, algorithms and detection progress of smartphone-based smart visual sensors.

Visual sensors possess significant potential for biogenic amine detection; however, to further advance their development and expand their applications in food, the following challenges and research areas need to be addressed: (1) Contemporary synthesis methods for the majority of materials continue to be intricate. Moreover, structural instability, low extinction coefficients, and inadequate biocompatibility continue to constrain the utilization of color-developing materials. The emphasis of future research should be on streamlined preparation methods, well-structured designs, and the application of composite modification strategies to produce eco-friendly materials that preserve the benefits of raw materials while achieving superior properties. (2) It is crucial to expand the application range of visual sensors under special conditions, such as developing new visual sensors with anti-freeze, anti-fog and other functions to reduce the impact of ice crystals and water vapor on the sensor in different environments like low-temperature transport or freezing. (3) Rapid detection is crucial for future on-site detection and preliminary screening of massive food samples. Focusing on the development of integrated methods with multiple functions, including sampling, extraction, and color development, is a key direction for adapting to on-site and large-scale sample detection. (4) The smartphone-based sensing platform possesses inherent limitations, and variations in the smartphone models employed across different studies may result in inaccuracies during subsequent data processing. Consequently, it is essential to create application software with an integrated parameter adjustment feature. Furthermore, to enhance integration with smartphones, machine learning algorithms must prioritize the development of lightweight models and the enhancement of interpretability. Integrate research findings from various fields, including materials science, food science, and biochemistry, into the design and enhancement of biogenic amine vision sensors in future advancements.
